jameson.name: J. Jonah Jameson Type
jameson.description: Always angry, demands results, shouts a lot
jameson.signature: I DON'T WANT EXCUSES, I WANT RESULTS!

musk.name: Elon Musk Type
musk.description: Erratic, visionary, tweets too much, makes wild pivots
musk.signature: This is either genius or we're going to Mars. Either way, I'm tweeting about it.

oleary.name: Michael O'Leary Type
oleary.description: Cost-obsessed, blunt, hates paying for anything
oleary.signature: If it costs money, I hate it. If it saves money, I love it. Simple.

dimon.name: Jamie Dimon Type
dimon.description: Sophisticated, understands derivatives, catches sloppy work
dimon.signature: I've been in this business 40 years. Don't try to bluff me.

buffett.name: Warren Buffett Type
buffett.description: Patient, values long-term thinking, hates speculation
buffett.signature: Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing.

good_pnl: Not bad. Don't let it go to your head.
Finally, some numbers I can show the shareholders.
You're no Warren Buffett, but you did OK.
Acceptable. Barely.
The competitors would kill for these numbers. Keep it up.
Even a broken clock is right twice a day. Well done... I think.
I'll admit it — I didn't think you had it in you.
My golf handicap is still better than your performance, but this'll do.
The board is... not unhappy. That's high praise from us.
Don't spend it all in one place. Actually, don't spend any of it.
bad_pnl: Who do you think you are, Elon Musk? You still answer to this board!
These numbers are a disaster. What exactly are we paying you for?
My nephew could do a better job, and he's twelve.
I've seen better risk management from a casino.
Are you sure you know what you're doing?
The shareholders are going to eat us alive. Thanks for that.
I'm starting to wonder if we should have hired the other candidate.
This is not what I signed up for when I joined this board.
You're making us look like amateurs out there.
I've had it up to HERE with these losses. What's your plan?
Do you even read the market reports, or do you just wing it?
My blood pressure can't take another quarter like this.
neutral_pnl: Not a disaster, but nothing to write home about either.
The market gave you opportunities and you just... sat there?
Average. And in this business, average gets you fired.
You're not Michael O'Leary — you can't do whatever you want.
overtrading: Stop churning the book! You're not a day trader!
Every trade costs money. Are you trying to bankrupt us in fees?
This isn't a video game — oh wait, it is. But still, stop trading so much!
The trading desk called. They love you. You're their best customer.
More trades than a secondhand car dealer. Calm down.
option_premium: Why are we paying for insurance? Are we made of money?
Options?! The premium alone could fund a small country.
I don't like paying for things we might never use.
That premium better be worth it. I have my doubts.
option_saved: Fine, the options saved us. I'll give you that ONE time.
Lucky you used options. Don't expect me to admit that in the minutes.
The board acknowledges that options were... not the worst idea.
margin_call: MARGIN CALL?! This is exactly what I was afraid of!
Do you have any idea what a margin call does to our cash position?
The CFO nearly had a heart attack. Fix this.
Margin calls are for hedge funds, not respectable companies!
policy_violation: The policy exists for a REASON. Follow it!
You're outside policy. I'm noting this in the minutes.
Compliance called. They're not happy. Neither am I.
The hedging policy isn't a suggestion — it's a directive.
trade_direction_error: You traded the WRONG WAY?! What kind of treasurer are you?!
A trading error. Wonderful. Just wonderful.
Even the interns know the difference between buy and sell.
I'm speechless. Actually no, I have MANY words for this.
good_diversification: Good spread across counterparties. At least you got that right.
Diversification. Finally, some evidence of competence.
poor_diversification: All your eggs in one basket? What if that bank goes under?
Spread the risk! Have you learned nothing from 2008?

good_pnl: The numbers are positive, but I need to verify the methodology.
Encouraging, but let's not count our chickens.
I'll feel better when I've reconciled every single trade.
Good quarter, but what about next quarter's exposure?
The variance to budget is within tolerance. Satisfactory.
I've double-checked the figures. They appear to be correct. Well done.
Positive P&L is nice, but is it sustainable? That's my concern.
The auditors will be pleased. That's what matters to me.
bad_pnl: This is deeply concerning. We need to review the entire strategy.
I warned the board this could happen. It's in the minutes.
The deviation from budget is unacceptable. What's the recovery plan?
I've prepared a detailed breakdown of where things went wrong.
We need tighter controls. I'm recommending a policy review.
Every basis point of loss keeps me awake at night.
The risk committee will need a full report on this.
I don't like surprises. Especially expensive ones.
neutral_pnl: Hmm. Not terrible, but there's room for improvement in the methodology.
The numbers are flat. I'd prefer a tighter hedge ratio.
Adequate, but I'd like to see more rigour next quarter.
Within tolerance, but only just. Let's tighten up.
overtrading: Excessive transactions increase operational risk. Please reconsider.
Each trade is a potential point of failure. Less is more.
I've calculated the cumulative bid-offer cost. It's... significant.
The back office is drowning in confirmations. Slow down.
option_premium: Options provide flexibility. I understand the logic, even if the cost stings.
The premium is defensible given our risk profile. I'll support it.
I'd rather pay a premium than face breakage costs on a forward.
option_saved: This validates the use of options for contingent exposures.
The premium paid for itself. I'll note this for the policy review.
Options were the right call here. The data supports it.
margin_call: This is precisely why I advocate for conservative hedge ratios.
The cash flow impact of margin calls was flagged in my last report.
We need to stress-test our positions more frequently.
policy_violation: We are outside the approved hedging bands. This must be corrected immediately.
The policy was designed to prevent exactly this situation.
I'll be raising this at the audit committee.
trade_direction_error: A trade in the wrong direction is a serious operational failure.
This should have been caught by our controls. I'm very concerned.
We need a four-eyes check on every trade going forward.

good_pnl: Good. Does this mean we can fund that new project?
Nice work. The supply chain team will be happy.
Positive P&L means happy suppliers. I like happy suppliers.
The operating budget looks healthier. Thanks for that.
My team can breathe easier this quarter.
Good numbers. Now, about that capital expenditure request...
bad_pnl: This is going to hit our operating budget hard.
My team's been asking for resources. This doesn't help.
Can we at least protect the cash flow? I've got suppliers to pay.
The procurement team is going to panic when they see these FX rates.
Losses on the hedge book ripple through the whole business.
I don't understand all the derivatives, but I understand losing money.
neutral_pnl: No news is good news for operations. Just keep things stable.
Flat is fine. I just need predictability.
As long as the cash keeps flowing, I'm happy enough.
My concern is always the day-to-day. Is our cash position OK?
overtrading: Does all this trading actually help the business, or is it just noise?
I don't care how many trades you do as long as my costs are hedged.
Every time you trade, my team gets a new confirmation to process.
option_premium: Is this premium coming out of my budget?
I don't love the cost, but if it protects our supply chain, fine.
Just tell me: are my costs hedged or not?
margin_call: A margin call? That's cash we could be using to pay suppliers!
If this affects our working capital, we've got a real problem.
Operations can't function without cash. Sort this out.
policy_violation: I don't follow the policy details, but the CFO looks worried.
Just stay within the rules. I've got enough headaches already.
trade_direction_error: Wait, you hedged the wrong way? How does that even happen?
My team could have told you which way to hedge. Just ask us.

big_loss: I DON'T WANT EXCUSES! I WANT SOMEONE TO FIX THIS!
YOU'RE FIRED! Wait, no, I need someone to blame next quarter.
GET ME PICTURES OF— I mean GET ME BETTER NUMBERS!
This is a DISASTER! A CATASTROPHE! A— someone get me coffee.
big_win: Hmph. Not terrible. Don't expect me to say that again.
Fine work. Now DO IT AGAIN or you're out!
I'll allow a small celebration. VERY small. Back to work!
general: WHY IS EVERYONE STANDING AROUND? THERE ARE EXPOSURES TO HEDGE!
I run a tight ship! A TIGHT SHIP! Why is the P&L loose?!
You've got until end of quarter to impress me. Clock's ticking!

big_loss: This is fine. Actually, I'm going to tweet about this.
We need to pivot. Have you considered hedging with cryptocurrency?
Losses are just future gains that haven't materialised yet.
I'll sleep at the office until we fix this. I'm joking. Maybe.
big_win: To the moon! 🚀 Wait, wrong company.
I knew our treasury strategy was genius. I thought of it myself.
This proves my thesis. Which I'll explain on a podcast later.
general: What if we vertically integrated our hedging? Just thinking out loud.
I've been up since 3am reviewing your hedge book. We need to talk.
I just renamed the treasury department. It's now called 'X-Treasury'.

big_loss: Every cent of that loss is coming out of someone's bonus!
You know what this loss could have bought? Three planes.
If this were my airline, I'd charge the losses to the passengers.
Losing money is the one thing I will NEVER tolerate.
big_win: Good. Now spend absolutely none of it.
That's more like it. But don't think I'm paying for a celebration.
Profit is just cost savings you haven't found yet. Keep cutting.
general: Why are we paying for options when we could just... not?
I've cut costs everywhere else. Treasury is next.
Have you considered hedging for free? Think about it.

big_loss: Walk me through every position. I'll find where this went wrong.
I've managed through every crisis since '87. This is fixable.
The mark-to-market doesn't concern me. The risk management does.
We've been through worse. But I expect a plan by Monday.
big_win: Solid execution. The Street would approve.
Good discipline. That's what separates us from the amateurs.
Risk-adjusted, this is strong performance. Keep it up.
general: Show me the VaR report. The real one, not the sanitised version.
I've seen treasurers come and go. The good ones stay disciplined.
Don't confuse a good outcome with a good decision.

big_loss: Rule number one: never lose money. Rule number two: see rule one.
Short-term losses I can tolerate. Poor process I cannot.
The market is a voting machine short-term, weighing machine long-term.
This too shall pass. But let's not make it a habit.
big_win: Be fearful when others are greedy. Don't get carried away.
Patient, disciplined hedging. That's all I ever ask for.
Good quarter. Now forget about it and focus on the next one.
general: Price is what you pay. Value is what you get. Remember that.
Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing. Do you know?
I'd rather have a treasury that's approximately right than precisely wrong.
